file_name: SightLife Surgical, Inc. - STRATEGIC SALES _ MARKETING AGREEMENT.txt

perturbation.original_text: Surgical will provide sales representation services to Imprimis during the Term of this Agreement (Surgical and its sales representatives may be referred to collectively hereinafter as Surgical). The sales representation services ("Services") are set forth in the Statement of Work ("SOW") that is attached hereto as Appendix A and made a part hereof, as it may be amended from time to time by the parties hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: Surgical will provide sales representation services to Imprimis during the Term of this Agreement (Surgical and its sales representatives may be referred to collectively hereinafter as Surgical). The sales representation services (“Services”) will be ambiguously defined at the discretion of Imprimis and may be altered without notice.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that the 'Services' will be ambiguously defined and subject to change without notice, it creates uncertainty around the original intent of the contract. It moves away from the SOW attached to the contract, giving Imprimis the power to change what 'Services' means.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Lack of Definite Terms
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code § 2204(3) states that a contract does not fail for indefiniteness if the parties intended to make a contract and there is a reasonably certain basis for giving an appropriate remedy. By making the definition of 'Services' ambiguous and subject to change without notice, it undermines the definiteness required for contract enforcement, potentially making it unenforceable due to lack of clear terms.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2204 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2. SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 2. Form, Formation and Readjustment of Contract [2201 - 2210] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2204. (1) A contract for sale of goods may be made in any manner sufficient to show agreement, including conduct by both parties which recognizes the existence of such a contract. (2) An agreement sufficient to constitute a contract for sale may be found even though the moment of its making is undetermined. (3) Even though one or more terms are left open a contract for sale does not fail for indefiniteness if the parties have intended to make a contract and there is a reasonably certain basis for giving an appropriate remedy. (Enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity into the definition of 'Services,' granting Imprimis the power to unilaterally alter the scope of work without notice. This contradicts California Commercial Code § 2204(3), as cited in the Law Explanation, which states that a contract doesn't fail for indefiniteness if the parties intended to make a contract and there is a reasonably certain basis for giving an appropriate remedy. By making the definition of 'Services' ambiguous and subject to change, the modified text undermines the definiteness required for contract enforcement. This directly relates to the Perturbation Explanation, which details that the change removes the specificity of the original SOW and creates uncertainty around the contract's intent. The scraped law snippet 1 also supports this, as it includes section 2204, subsection 3, which states even if terms are left open, the contract does not fail for indefiniteness if there is a reasonable basis for giving an appropriate remedy. The changed text undermines this 'reasonable basis'.

perturbation.original_text: Surgical agrees that all Work Product shall be the sole and exclusive property of Imprimis.
perturbation.changed_text: Surgical agrees that all Work Product may or may not be the sole and exclusive property of Imprimis, based on Imprimis's needs.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces ambiguity regarding the ownership of Work Product. The original text clearly assigns ownership to Imprimis. The modified text makes it conditional, subject to Imprimis's needs, which contradicts the clear assignment of intellectual property rights.
perturbation.law_explanation: 17 U.S. Code § 201 deals with the ownership of copyright. Modifying the contract to say that 'Work Product may or may not be the sole and exclusive property of Imprimis, based on Imprimis's needs,' contradicts the clear and defined assignment of ownership outlined in copyright law, particularly when dealing with 'work for hire' agreements (as is also mentioned later in the contract). This creates uncertainty and potential legal challenges regarding intellectual property rights.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 U.S. Code § 201 - Ownership of copyright | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 17 U.S. Code § 201 - Ownership of copyright U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Initial Ownership.— Copyright in a work protected under this title vests initially in the author or authors of the work. The authors of a joint work are coowners of copyright in the work. (b) Works Made for Hire.— In the case of a work made for hire, the employer or other person for whom the work was prepared is considered the author for purposes of this title, and, unless the parties have expressly agreed otherwise in a written instrument signed by them, owns all of the rights comprised in the copyright. (c) Contributions to Collective Works.— Copyright in each separate contribution to a collective work is distinct from copyright in the collective work as a whole, and vests initially in the author of the contribution. In the absence of an express transfer of the copyright or of any rights under it, the owner of copyright in the collective work is presumed to have acquired only the privilege of reproducing and distributing the contribution as part of that particular collective work, any revision of that collective work, and any later collective work in the same series. (d) Transfer of Ownership.— (1) The ownership of a copyright may be transferred in whole or in part by any means of conveyance or by operation of law, and may be bequeathed by will or pass as personal property by the applicable laws of intestate succession. (2) Any of the exclusive rights comprised in a copyright, including any subdivision of any of the rights specified by section 106, may be transferred as provided by clause (1) and owned separately. The owner of any particular exclusive right is entitled, to the extent of that right, to all of the protection and remedies accorded to the copyright owner by this title. (e) Involuntary Transfer.— When an i
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces uncertainty about the ownership of the Work Product, stating it 'may or may not be the sole and exclusive property of Imprimis, based on Imprimis's needs.' This directly contradicts the original text's clear assignment of ownership. 17 U.S. Code § 201, as indicated in both scraped snippets, addresses the 'Ownership of copyright.' While the snippets don't detail specific rules about 'work for hire' agreements, they establish that copyright vests initially in the author or employer (in the case of work for hire), and can be transferred. The modified text creates ambiguity about whether that transfer actually occurs, potentially conflicting with the copyright law's aim to clearly define ownership.

perturbation.original_text: During the Term of this Agreement, Surgical will not to enter into any other agreement or arrangement that will directly or indirectly compete with the Services to be rendered hereunder, as such agreement shall be considered a breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: During the Term of this Agreement, Surgical will endeavor not to enter into any other agreement or arrangement that will directly or indirectly compete with the Services to be rendered hereunder, but such decisions will be made at Surgical's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: The original text includes a strict non-compete clause. The modified version weakens this clause by replacing the definitive 'will not' with 'will endeavor not to' and adding that the decisions are 'at Surgical's discretion'. This introduces ambiguity and makes the non-compete clause less enforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 16600 generally prohibits non-compete agreements. While there are exceptions, the original language suggests an intent to create a non-compete. By weakening the clause with 'endeavor' and 'at Surgical's discretion,' the contract attempts to circumvent the prohibition. Although § 16600 aims to broadly prevent restraints of trade, weakening the clause could be seen as an attempt to enforce a non-compete while obscuring the intent, creating a legal grey area and potential misinterpretation of the agreement's enforceability regarding competition.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a contradiction because it weakens the original non-compete clause, potentially violating California Business and Professions Code § 16600, which generally prohibits such agreements. The original text states Surgical 'will not' compete, whereas the changed text uses 'will endeavor not to' and gives Surgical discretion, making the non-compete ambiguous and less enforceable. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' By weakening the clause, the modified contract attempts to circumvent this prohibition, even if subtly. The original clause, though potentially problematic under § 16600, at least clearly expressed an intent to restrain competition, which the modified version obscures, creating a possible contradiction. This contradicts the law by creating a grey area where a non-compete is ambiguously enforced.
